attrs.alt: Commit traceability chain: git commit carries footer fields linking to a prompt file, whose SHA-256 hash acts as a stable identifier, which in turn links to an eval run CI artifact and the resulting pass rate stored in a registry.

content.text: The diagram shows the complete traceability chain for an AI system commit. A

carries footer fields that point at the exact prompt file version used, its SHA-256 hash, the CI eval run that tested that prompt, and the resulting pass rate. When a production incident occurs, following this chain backward surfaces the precise prompt version and eval evidence within seconds.
The Traceability Problem in AI Systems
Traditional software bugs are relatively easy to attribute: a stack trace names the file and line, and

identifies the commit that introduced that code. AI systems break this attribution model in two ways. First, behavior is determined not only by code but also by prompt files, model parameters, and eval thresholds — all of which can change independently and often in the same commit as code. Second, the effect of a prompt change is probabilistic and task-specific: a wording change that improves one class of inputs may degrade another class that no one thought to test.

The result is that AI teams in production frequently face questions they cannot answer with standard tooling: Which prompt version was serving users between 14:00 and 15:30 UTC on the day of the incident? Did the eval pass rate for the affected task type drop before or after the deploy? Who approved the last safety-relevant prompt change? Which commit changed the model from one version to another?

Commit message traceability fields address this gap by embedding metadata directly in the git history — the one artifact that already links code, author, and timestamp. Because every deploy is based on a commit, and every commit can carry a structured footer, the commit log becomes a queryable audit trail that covers prompts, evals, model versions, and reviewer approvals without requiring a separate database.
footer field records the SHA-256 digest of every prompt file modified in the commit, in the format

. This is distinct from the git commit SHA, which hashes the entire tree including source code. The prompt hash is stable across refactors that do not touch the prompt text: renaming a Python function does not change the prompt hash, making it a reliable identity for the prompt independently of surrounding code.
Computing a SHA-256 digest of a prompt file is one line in any language. In Python:
distinguishes hash algorithm if the policy is later migrated. A commit touching two prompt files carries two
Use the full 64-character hex digest, never a truncated prefix. Truncation risks collisions across thousands of commits over the life of a project.
Hash raw bytes, not a decoded string. Encoding-sensitive whitespace (CRLF vs LF) creates different hashes for the same logical content if bytes are decoded and re-encoded before hashing.
Store the path alongside the hash:
so that the registry lookup can locate the file without parsing every prompt in the repository.
For multi-file commits affecting several agents, repeat the field once per modified prompt file rather than combining hashes into a single aggregate.
Linking Commits to Eval Run IDs
footer field carries a URI pointing to the CI eval artifact for this commit. The format depends on the CI provider. For GitHub Actions the convention is
. The URI scheme encodes the provider, allowing tooling to open the correct URL without per-provider logic in every script.

This field is set by the CI pipeline itself, not by the developer. The pre-commit hook prepares the other fields; the pipeline runs eval, then amends the commit message footer with the run ID before tagging the final artifact. This design means the
field is always accurate — it cannot be faked by a developer who writes an ID that was never executed.
The eval run ID must resolve to a publicly accessible artifact (within the organization) so that auditors can open the link and inspect the full eval log.
If the eval run is skipped (no prompt files changed), the field is omitted rather than set to a placeholder. Absence is meaningful and parseable.
When re-running eval after a flaky failure, the
field is updated to the new run ID. Retaining the stale run ID would mislead audit queries.
Linking Commits to Eval Pass Rates
field records the percentage of eval test cases that passed, written as a decimal to two places:

(the percent sign is omitted so scripts can parse the value directly as a float). This field is population-level, not per-task. Per-task breakdowns live in the eval artifact itself; the commit footer carries the headline number needed to spot regressions in
output without opening the CI system.
Tracking this number in commit history creates a lightweight regression chart: a

lines gives a time series of pass rates that can be graphed or analyzed without any external database. Teams often pipe this into a monitoring dashboard as a secondary signal alongside production metrics.
Use a consistent decimal format so that numerical comparison and charting scripts do not need format detection logic.

If multiple task types have distinct pass rates, report the worst-performing task's rate in the footer, since a regression in one task type can be masked by high-performing tasks when an average is used.
The CI gate should block merges when
drops below the configured threshold, providing an automated quality floor independent of human review.

The complete set of standardized footer fields for an AI system commit covers six concerns: prompt identity, eval evidence, quality rate, safety approval, agent scope, and model version. Each field follows the
format used by git trailers (RFC 2822 compliant), which means
A pre-commit hook eliminates the manual step of computing and inserting

lines. The hook runs before the commit is finalized, inspects the staged files for any paths under the prompt directory, hashes them, and appends the fields to the commit message file. This ensures the footer is never omitted through oversight.
The hook hashes the staged blob (via

), not the working-tree file. This is the correct choice: if a developer has unsaved edits, they are not part of the commit and should not influence the hash. Using the staged content guarantees the hash and the committed content are always in sync.
Commit Message Template with Traceability Fields

template pre-populates the commit message editor with the expected structure. Developers fill in the body and leave the trailer keys as prompts. The pre-commit hook fills in the hash values automatically; developers manually add the other fields or leave them blank when not applicable.

# .gitmessage — commit message template for AI system repositories
# Configure: git config commit.template .gitmessage
#
# Format: <type>(<scope>): <subject>   (50 chars max)
# Types: feat | fix | prompt | eval | model | refactor | docs | ci
#
# ---- SUBJECT (required) -----------------------------------------------


# ---- BODY (what changed and why, 72-char lines) -----------------------


# ---- TRACEABILITY FOOTER (filled by hook + CI) ------------------------
# Prompt-Hash:     <auto-filled by prepare-commit-msg hook>
# Eval-Run:        <auto-filled by CI pipeline after eval job>
# Eval-Pass-Rate:  <auto-filled by CI pipeline after eval job>
# Safety-Reviewer: <email of approver — required if prompt type>
# Agent-Name:      <agent slug — required for multi-agent repos>
# Model-Version:   <model-id — required if model parameter changed>
#
# Example complete footer:
# Prompt-Hash:     prompts/classify.txt:sha256:e3b0c44298fc1c149afb4c8
# Eval-Run:        gh-actions://runs/12890443
# Eval-Pass-Rate:  94.20
# Safety-Reviewer: alice@example.com
# Agent-Name:      classifier-v2
# Model-Version:   claude-3-5-haiku-20241022
. The commented lines do not appear in the commit message; they serve only as reminders in the editor.
A verification script checks that every commit bearing a
footer actually refers to a hash that matches the current file content on that commit's tree. This catches cases where the hook was bypassed (via
) or where the file was modified after the hook ran.

Run this script in CI as a separate lint job after checkout. Any mismatch is a signal that either the hook was bypassed or the commit was amended after the hook ran. Both are policy violations in a team that uses traceability for safety reviews.
Querying Traceability: Finding Which Prompt Caused an Incident
flag searches commit message bodies, including footer fields. Combined with date filters, it locates the commits that were live during any incident window with no external tooling required.
Find all commits carrying a specific prompt hash:
Find all prompt-touching commits in a date range:
Extract all pass rates as a time series:
Find which commit last changed the model version:
Identify commits in the incident window that lacked a safety reviewer:

These queries work on any machine with a clone of the repository, without access to CI dashboards or external tracking systems. This independence from external services is a critical property: during an incident, CI may be unavailable or slow. Git is local and fast.

Eval results should be stored as versioned artifacts in CI, not ephemeral logs. A CI job that runs the eval suite uploads the results as a named artifact before setting the exit code, so that even failed eval runs leave a retrievable record. The artifact name embeds the commit SHA:
, providing an independent lookup path when the commit footer is the starting point.
Store eval results in a structured JSON format with per-task breakdown, timestamp, model version, and the prompt hashes used for that run. This makes the artifact self-contained for audit purposes.
Retain artifacts for at least 90 days. Incident retrospectives often surface weeks after the event; artifacts that expired before the retrospective block root cause analysis.
For large eval suites, also store a summary JSON with just the headline numbers. The
URI should point to this summary when available, with the full artifact accessible via a known naming convention.
Tag releases with the eval artifact reference so that release notes can link directly to the eval evidence for that version.

field records the email address of the person who approved a prompt change with safety implications. Safety-relevant changes include modifying system prompts that constrain harmful outputs, adjusting refusal thresholds, or altering how the agent communicates its capabilities and limitations.

This field is not a substitute for the organization's code review process — it does not replace a GitHub pull request approval. It is a machine-readable record that the safety review occurred and who performed it, so that compliance audits and incident investigations can identify the approval chain without re-reading every pull request manually.
Define in team policy which prompt changes trigger the
requirement. Vague definitions lead to inconsistent application.
The pre-commit hook can enforce presence of the field for commits to files matching a safety-critical path pattern, blocking the commit if the field is absent.
Use an email address rather than a GitHub username so that the record remains meaningful if the repository is migrated to a different provider.
For asynchronous reviews, the reviewer can submit the
trailer as a comment on the pull request, which is then extracted by CI and added to the commit message automatically.

footer records the model identifier that was in effect after a commit, not the model that existed before. For commits that do not change the model, this field is omitted — its absence means the model version inherited from the most recent commit that did carry the field.
This design keeps the history compact while remaining queryable:
produces a sparse list of model change events. Between any two events, the model version is stable and known.
Use the full model identifier including date suffix (e.g.,
) rather than a short alias. Aliases are mutable; the full ID is immutable.
If the same commit changes both a prompt and a model, include both
. The eval results for that commit must be interpreted as reflecting the combination.
When a model is deprecated and replaced, the deprecation commit should carry a
field with the replacement, creating a clean transition point in the audit log.
Agent Name Scoping for Multi-Agent Repos
In a monorepo hosting multiple agents, a
field alone is insufficient to determine which agent was affected. The
field scopes all other traceability fields to a specific agent, enabling queries like "show me all prompt changes to the classifier agent over the past 30 days" without parsing the path in the
Agent names should be stable slugs with no spaces, following the same convention as directory names:
When a commit touches multiple agents, include one
line per agent, in the same order as the corresponding
field can be set as a default in the
template rather than requiring manual entry on every commit.
Tooling: commitizen Plugin for Traceability Fields

) is a CLI tool for enforcing conventional commit messages. Its plugin system allows adding custom questions to the interactive commit flow, which appear alongside the standard type/scope/subject prompts. A traceability plugin prompts for the fields that cannot be auto-computed, validates the format, and writes them to the commit message — removing the need for developers to remember the exact field names and formats.
for guided entry, with the pre-commit hook appending hash values automatically.
Audit Trail: Reconstructing the Full History of a Prompt
To reconstruct the complete history of a prompt file — every version, its hash at that version, the eval pass rate each version achieved, and who reviewed it — combine
on the prompt file with the trailer extraction. The following workflow produces a structured timeline:
to get every commit that modified the file, in chronological order.
For each entry, retrieve the prompt content at that version:
. This allows side-by-side diff between any two versions without needing a registry.
URI with the CI artifact store to retrieve per-task breakdown for each version, providing a complete quality profile over the file's lifetime.
Output the timeline as structured JSON or a Markdown table for inclusion in an incident report or compliance audit.
Incident Response: Root Cause via Commit Traceability

Commit traceability transforms incident response from a narrative reconstruction into a structured lookup. The following procedure applies when a quality regression or safety issue is observed in production:
Identify the deploy timestamp from infrastructure logs. Map it to a commit SHA using the deploy audit log or the tag on the deployed artifact.
to read the commit footer. Extract
of the offending commit with the previous commit that carried the same field. A drop identifies the introduction of the regression.
URI to inspect the per-task breakdown and identify which task type regressed. This narrows the affected prompt file even when multiple prompts were changed.
to read the exact prompt that was live during the incident. Diff it against the previous version to identify the specific change that caused the regression.
to determine whether the change passed review. If the field is absent for a safety-relevant change, document the process failure alongside the technical root cause.

This procedure is deterministic and repeatable. It produces the same root cause analysis on the tenth re-run as on the first, because all the evidence is in the git log and the CI artifact store — neither of which changes after the fact.
Embed traceability in the git commit footer using
fields. These fields make the git log the primary audit source for AI system changes.
hook that hashes staged blobs — not working-tree files — so the hash always matches what was committed.
after the eval job completes. This prevents developers from manually entering stale or fabricated eval references.

Verify hash integrity periodically with a script that re-computes SHA-256 from the committed tree and compares against the footer. Mismatches indicate hook bypass and should be treated as policy violations.

During incident response, the git log provides everything needed to identify the offending prompt version, the eval state at the time of the deploy, and who approved the change — without depending on CI systems that may be unavailable during an outage.
Use commitizen plugins to guide developers through the traceability fields they must supply manually (safety reviewer, model version), reducing the cognitive overhead that leads to omissions.
